past_medical_history: Episode of right arm and torso numbness lasting 3 weeks, approximately 6 months ago, evaluated by PCP and attributed to possible cervical radiculopathy; no imaging was performed at that time
Mild iron deficiency anemia, diagnosed 2 years ago, resolved with supplementation
family_history: No family history of multiple sclerosis or other neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Social drinker, 1-2 glasses of wine per week
social_history.living_situation: Lives with partner in an apartment
social_history.exercise: Regular yoga 3 times per week, recreational hiking on weekends

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Fund of knowledge appropriate. Affect is anxious but cooperative.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity reduced in left eye to 20/80 (right eye 20/20). Left relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). Red desaturation in the left eye. Fundoscopy shows mild left optic disc pallor. CN III-XII otherwise intact. No facial asymmetry. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and interossei. Lower extremities: 4+/5 left hip flexion, otherwise 5/5 bilaterally. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense in both lower extremities to the ankles. Decreased light touch and pinprick over the right arm and right lateral trunk (T4-T8 dermatomes), corresponding to the prior episode. Temperature sensation intact. Proprioception intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally. Achilles 3+ bilaterally. Left plantar response extensor (positive Babinski sign). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly impaired on the left with slight overshoot. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly unsteady tandem gait. Romberg sign positive. Regular gait mildly wide-based. Able to walk on heels and toes without difficulty.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive (electric shock sensation down the spine with neck flexion). No meningismus. EDSS estimated at 2.5.

patient.chief_complaint: Painful vision loss in the left eye over 3 days
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Sarah Lindgren is a 28-year-old right-handed woman who presents with a 3-day history of progressive vision loss in her left eye accompanied by retro-orbital pain that worsens with eye movement. She first noticed blurring in her left visual field while reading on her tablet three evenings ago and initially attributed it to fatigue after a long day at work. Over the following 24 hours the vision in her left eye continued to deteriorate, and she noticed that colors, particularly reds and oranges, appeared washed out when viewed through the left eye compared to the right. She also developed a dull aching pain behind her left eye that is exacerbated by lateral and upward gaze. By the morning of presentation, she could only see vague shapes with the left eye and became alarmed, prompting her partner to bring her to the emergency department. On further questioning, she reports that approximately six months ago she experienced a three-week episode of numbness and tingling involving her right arm and the right side of her trunk, which she describes as a band-like sensation around her ribcage. She saw her primary care physician at that time who suspected a pinched nerve and recommended over-the-counter analgesics. The numbness gradually improved but she notes that she still has intermittent tingling in the right hand, particularly when she is tired or overheated. She also reports that over the past several months she has experienced unusual fatigue that is disproportionate to her activity level, and she has occasionally noticed that her left leg feels heavy after prolonged walking or exercise, though she has not fallen. She denies fevers, weight loss, rash, joint pain, oral ulcers, or other systemic symptoms. She denies any prior episodes of vision loss, diplopia, dysphagia, or bowel and bladder dysfunction.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, predominantly along the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 5 mm to 18 mm

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, ovoid morphology perpendicular to ventricles (Dawson fingers pattern)

findings.location: Juxtacortical white matter, right frontal and left parietal lobes

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Avid enhancement of left optic nerve
signal_characteristics.T2_fat_sat: Hyperintense signal within left optic nerve

findings.mass_effect: Mild swelling of the left optic nerve

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, right lateral ventricle body

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Open ring enhancement pattern, ring open toward cortex
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense core with surrounding hyperintense halo

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4 level, dorsal columns
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in length

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (lesion spans less than 1 vertebral segment)
mri.impression: Multiple demyelinating lesions involving the periventricular white matter, juxtacortical regions, infratentorial structures (left middle cerebellar peduncle), left optic nerve, and cervical spinal cord. Two lesions demonstrate gadolinium enhancement, indicating active inflammation. The findings satisfy the 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord involvement) and dissemination in time (simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions). Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 153 mEq/L (H), Chloride 107 mEq/L (H), Vitamin D, 25-hydroxy 21 ng/mL (L).

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 6 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 8 cells/microL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 40.9 mg/dL (reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 56.9 mg/dL (reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.67 (serum glucose 92 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 6 bands unique to CSF, not present in serum. IgG index: 0.82 (elevated; reference: <0.70). IgG synthesis rate: 12.4 mg/day (elevated; reference: <8.0 mg/day). Myelin basic protein: 4.8 ng/mL (mildly elevated; reference: <4.0 ng/mL). Cytology: No malignant cells.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left eye P100 latency 128 ms ms (H), Left eye P100 amplitude 3.2 microV microV (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Left eye P100 latency: 128 ms ms (H)
Left eye P100 amplitude: 3.2 microV microV (L)
Interocular P100 latency difference: 29 ms ms (H)

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C3-C4, dorsal columns
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in length

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T6-T7, lateral columns, right-sided
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in length

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (all lesions span less than one vertebral segment)
Normal conus medullaris and cauda equina
output.impression: Two short-segment spinal cord lesions identified: one at C3-C4 (non-enhancing, chronic) and one at T6-T7 (faintly enhancing, likely subacute). Lesion characteristics are typical of demyelinating disease (MS pattern: short segment, peripheral location, less than half cross-sectional area). Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Peripapillary RNFL thickness (average) 72 micrometers micrometers (H), RNFL temporal quadrant 48 micrometers micrometers (H), Ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) 68 micrometers micrometers (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GCIPL): 68 micrometers micrometers (H)

findings.size: Multiple lesions, unchanged compared to prior study

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement (previously enhancing right periventricular lesion no longer enhances)

findings.location: Right periventricular white matter, posterior horn of lateral ventricle

volumetrics.resolving_lesions: 2 previously enhancing lesions no longer enhance

additional_observations: No new spinal cord lesions identified on sagittal cervical images
Previously noted lesions are stable in size
No evidence of progressive brain atrophy
output.impression: Follow-up MRI at 3 months demonstrates one new small enhancing lesion in the right periventricular white matter, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity despite corticosteroid treatment of the acute relapse. Prior enhancing lesions have resolved. Clinical correlation recommended.

contraindications: Not recommended in patients with known severe lymphopenia (lymphocyte count <500 cells/microL)
warnings: Monitor CBC including lymphocyte count before initiation and every 6 months
Flushing and GI side effects common in first month, may be reduced by taking with food
Risk of PML in patients with prolonged lymphopenia — withhold if lymphocytes <500 for >6 months
Consider pregnancy planning — recommend contraception during treatment
alternatives: Glatiramer acetate (lower efficacy, injection-based, favorable safety profile)

results.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria
results.authors: Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, et al.
results.key_findings: Revised criteria allow CSF oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time. Symptomatic and asymptomatic MRI lesions can both be used. Cortical lesions can substitute for juxtacortical lesions.

results.title: MRI criteria for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: MAGNIMS consensus guidelines
results.authors: Filippi M, Rocca MA, Ciccarelli O, et al.
results.key_findings: Dissemination in space requires at least 1 T2 lesion in at least 2 of 4 CNS areas: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT), or alternatively, DIS plus CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. DIS requires one or more T2 lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic locations. DIT can be demonstrated by simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, or new T2/enhancing lesions on follow-up MRI. In this patient, DIS is met by lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord regions, and DIT is met by the simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions.

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative, short-segment spinal cord lesions rather than longitudinally extensive, periventricular Dawson fingers morphology typical of MS, not NMOSD
MOG antibodies negative, optic disc pallor rather than prominent edema, CSF oligoclonal bands (less common in MOGAD), periventricular lesion pattern typical of MS
Normal ESR/CRP, negative ANA, Dawson fingers morphology inconsistent with vasculitis, young female demographic more typical of MS
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no systemic findings (pulmonary, skin, joints), no elevated ACE, lesion distribution classic for MS
No migraine history, lesions periventricular with Dawson fingers (not subcortical), enhancing lesions and CSF oligoclonal bands not seen with migraine

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including prior neurological symptoms suggesting prior demyelinating events (dissemination in time)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History of right arm and trunk numbness 6 months ago consistent with prior myelitis or sensory relapse

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with attention to optic nerve function, long tract signs, and sensory levels
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left RAPD, reduced left visual acuity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign, positive Lhermitte sign, sensory deficits in prior relapse distribution

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and cervical spine with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple demyelinating lesions meeting McDonald 2017 DIS criteria with enhancing lesions meeting DIT criteria

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, NMO-IgG, and MOG antibodies to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except low vitamin D; negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present, elevated IgG index, mild lymphocytic pleocytosis

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for acute optic neuritis
Discuss diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS with patient and counsel on disease-modifying therapy options
Initiate disease-modifying therapy (e.g., dimethyl fumarate, ocrelizumab, or natalizumab based on shared decision-making)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selected DMT appropriate for patient profile and disease activity

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to document baseline optic nerve function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in left eye confirming demyelinating optic neuropathy

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI of thoracic spine with contrast to complete spinal cord assessment
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Additional spinal cord lesion at T6-T7

optimal_actions.action: Supplement vitamin D (2000-5000 IU daily) given low levels
Schedule follow-up MRI in 3 months to establish new baseline on DMT

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium to evaluate for demyelinating lesions and confirm dissemination in space and time
Perform lumbar puncture to assess for oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
Check NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibodies) and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Treat acute optic neuritis with IV methylprednisolone
Initiate disease-modifying therapy after confirming MS diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Do not start chronic oral corticosteroids as maintenance therapy for MS (not effective and harmful long-term)
Do not perform brain biopsy — diagnosis is achievable with non-invasive criteria
Do not delay disease-modifying therapy while waiting for spontaneous remission
Do not prescribe carbamazepine or other sodium channel blockers for optic neuritis (may worsen conduction in demyelinated nerves acutely)
Do not initiate natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
key_reasoning_points: The combination of acute optic neuritis with a prior episode of limb numbness establishes clinical dissemination in time
MRI demonstrates dissemination in space across at least 3 of 4 characteristic MS locations (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord)
Simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on MRI provides radiographic dissemination in time, satisfying 2017 McDonald criteria
CSF oligoclonal bands provide additional evidence of intrathecal immune activation and can substitute for DIT under 2017 McDonald criteria
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which have different treatment approaches
The Lhermitte sign (electric sensation with neck flexion) is a classic symptom of cervical cord demyelination in MS
Relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) is the hallmark physical finding of unilateral optic neuropathy
Early initiation of high-efficacy DMT is recommended given evidence of active inflammation (enhancing lesions) and multiple relapses

metadata.mcdonald_criteria_met: 2017 McDonald criteria for DIS and DIT both satisfied
metadata.educational_value: Classic presentation of RRMS with optic neuritis, prior sensory relapse, and diagnostic MRI/CSF findings
